rows.label: statement
rows.note: Note: Irregular verbs do not take '-ed'. Learn the past simple form of irregular verbs.

content.subtitle: Use
items: Single completed actions: Tom and I played a game of chess and he won.
Habits in the past: Did you collect stamps when you were younger?
Permanent situations in the past: A famous footballer lived in our house before we bought it.
General truths and facts about the past: Crosswords didn't become popular until the 1930s.
The main events in a story: The referee blew the whistle and Simon passed the ball to James.

content.subtitle: Emphatic form
content.description: We can also use did in past simple statements for emphasis.
examples: I did win. Who told you I didn't?

content.subtitle: Use
items: Actions happening at a particular moment in the past: At five o'clock, I was reading my new book.
Temporary situations in the past: Greg was living in London at the time.
Annoying past habits (usually with always): When we were young, my brother was always borrowing my toys.
Actions in progress over a period of time: Daniel was playing video games all morning yesterday.
Two actions in progress at the same time: Were Ulla and her friends playing Monopoly while we were playing Draughts?
Background information in a story: The sun was shining and the birds were singing.

content.subtitle: Watch out!
content.description: When one action in the past interrupts another action in progress, we use the past simple and the past continuous together. I was playing on my computer when it suddenly crashed.
notes: We do not use the past continuous for regular or repeated actions in the past. ✓ When we were on holiday, we played volleyball every day.
We do not usually use stative verbs in continuous tenses.

content.subtitle: Use
items: Situations and states before the past: We'd lived next to the gym for a couple of months before I decided to join.
Completed actions before a moment in the past: I'd already bought the computer game when I saw it was cheaper.
Completed actions where the important thing is the result at a moment in the past: We didn't feel like playing Scrabble because we had just finished a long game of Monopoly.

content.subtitle: Notes
items: There is often little or no difference in meaning between the past perfect simple and the past simple.
After we have used the past perfect simple once, we often then use the past simple instead.
Phrases such as It was the first/second/etc time ... are followed by the past perfect simple: It was the second time I'd been on a plane.

content.subtitle: Use
items: Actions continuing up to a moment in the past: When you saw us, we had been running for six miles – and we still had a mile to go!
Actions stopping just before a moment in the past: Sarah looked tired because she had been exercising all morning.

content.subtitle: Use
items: Past habits, particularly for the distant past: When I was very young, my grandfather would take me to the park to play.

content.subtitle: Watch out!
content.description: We don't often use would in questions or negative statements with this meaning. In negative statements, we can use would never.

rows.label: negative
rows.left: didn't use to / never used to / used not to

content.subtitle: Use
items: Past habits and states, particularly for the distant past: My mother used to play a lot of squash before I was born.

content.subtitle: Watch out!
content.description: To talk about a past state, we can use used to, but not would.
notes: ✓ We used to have a house that was right next to the park.

content.subtitle: Use
items: A situation that is familiar or no longer strange: I didn't like being the goalkeeper at first but now I'm used to it.
To talk about the process of becoming familiar with something, we use get used to: I'm gradually getting used to being in a new team.

content.subtitle: Watch out!
content.description: Don't confuse **be/get used to + -ing** with **used to + infinitive**.
notes: ✓ I'm used to getting up early. (= It's no longer strange for me.)
✓ I used to get up early. (= I did this in the past, but I don't anymore.)

sections.title: A: Past simple vs. Past continuous
sections.instructions: Circle the correct word or phrase.

items.sentence: I **saw / was seeing** Maria for the first time at Ray's birthday party.
Richard **watched / was watching** TV when the phone rang.
When we were on holiday, we **went / were going** to the café almost every day.
Denise **practised / was practising** the song every day until she could sing it perfectly.
The phone was engaged when I called. Who did you **talk / were you talking** to?
Mr Connors **owned / was owning** two houses and a villa in the south of France.
I **did / was doing** my homework as soon as I got home from school.
A car came round the corner and I **jumped / was jumping** out of the way.
When my dad met my mum, he **worked / was working** as a bus driver.
I **got / was getting** up at six o'clock every morning last week!
My cousin and I **played / were playing** on the computer when there was a power cut.
No, that's not right. I **did pass / was passing** the test. I got a B.

sections.title: B: Complete using the correct form
sections.instructions: Complete using the correct form of the verb in brackets.

items.sentence: Where (you / go) ...... when I saw you on the bus last night?
(you / enjoy) ...... the film?
When we shared a room, Zoë (always / take) ...... my things. It was so annoying!
When I went to get the tickets, I realised (not / have) ...... any money.
When I was young, we (go) ...... to France every year on holiday.
Elvis (become) ...... famous for the song Blue Suede Shoes.
When you rang last night, I (work) ...... in the garden so I didn't hear the phone.
I (hear) ...... from Davina last night. She says hello.
The old man (appear) ...... to be very tired and he slowly sat down.
We (throw) ...... a surprise party for my brother last Saturday.

sections.title: C: Past simple vs. Past perfect
sections.instructions: Circle the correct word or phrase.

items.sentence: I (1) **just left / had just left** for school when I saw the postman.
he (2) **gave / had given** it to me.
I (3) **read / had read** it during maths.
it (4) **made / had made** me laugh.
I almost (5) **got / had got** in trouble!
I (6) **had / had had** my first judo lesson yesterday.
I (7) **was / had been** late for the lesson.
when I (8) **got / had got** there, I suddenly realised I (9) **left / had left** my judo suit at home!
So I (10) **went / had gone** all the way home.
when I (11) **got / had got** back, the lesson (12) **already began / had already begun**.
I (13) **learned / had learned** how to do some basic throws.
The last time I (14) **spoke / had spoken** to you, you (15) **talked / had talked** about giving it up.
What (16) **did you decide / had you decided**?

sections.title: D: Past perfect simple & continuous
sections.instructions: Complete using the correct form of the verb in brackets.

items.sentence: By the time he died, Beethoven ...... (WRITE) nine symphonies.
We chose the Hotel Rio because we ...... (STAY) there before.
We ...... (WAIT) for over an hour when the train finally arrived.
I was completely out of breath because I ....... (RUN)
I ...... (SEE) the film before, so I knew how it ended.
When he got married, I ...... (KNOW) Chris for about two years.
Johnson ...... (GET) ready for the race for six months and finally the big moment came.
Vivian ...... (HAVE) computer lessons for very long so she wasn't sure how to use the Internet.
Holly ...... (EAT) oysters before, so she wasn't sure what to do with them.
I ...... (LISTEN) to my new CD for a few minutes when the CD player started making a funny noise.
My brother and I ...... swimming almost every day last summer.
We ...... when someone knocked at the door.
When the robbery happened, the security guard .......
Jack ...... chess before so I showed him what to do.
I wasn't sure how Belinda would react because I ...... her long.
Ian ...... at the factory long when he was made a manager.
I wanted to say goodbye to Jerry, but he .......
When we got to the airport, I realised I ...... my passport at home!

sections.title: F: Error correction (verb forms)
sections.instructions: Each of the sentences has an incorrect verb form. Rewrite it correctly.

items.sentence: I had paint on my shoes because I'd **painted** my bedroom all morning.
I missed the start of the film because I **buy** popcorn.
It was obvious that Bill **has worked** because he was very tired when I saw him.
We had been tidying the garden for hours and I **was needing** a rest.
When the bus **was arriving**, we missed it because we were talking.
During the Christmas holiday, I **was eating** too much and **watching** too much TV!
Julian **was learning** all about computer games by the time he was six.
My grandfather **was owning** a hotel by the beach until he sold it last year.

sections.title: G: Would, used to, be/get used to
sections.instructions: Circle the correct word or phrase.

items.sentence: When she was a girl, my mum **would / used to** live in a village.
I really can't **be / get** used to having a new baby brother.
People **would / are used** to die of diseases in the past that we can cure today.
There **would / used to** be a cinema on this corner, but they knocked it down.
It was strange at first, but I'm used to **play / playing** the bagpipes now.
Didn't you **use to / be** used to have blonde hair?
Christopher was **being / getting** used to the idea of joining the army.
People never **would / used to** be so worried about crime in this area.

sections.title: H: Key word transformation
sections.instructions: Complete each second sentence using the word given, so that it has a similar meaning to the first sentence. Write between two and five words in each gap.

items.sentence:
People walked more fifty years ago than they do now. **would**
Fifty years ago, ...... than they do now.

My parents wouldn't let me stay out late when I was young. **used**
My parents ...... let me stay out late when I was young.

Did you know that Carol played basketball for her country? **to**
Did you know that Carol ...... basketball for her country?

Sending messages around the world instantly is no longer unusual. **got**
We ...... messages around the world instantly.

When he was a teacher, my dad often used to get home quite late. **would**
When he was a teacher, my dad ...... quite late.

Do you think you could learn to live without your mobile phone? **used**
Do you think you could ...... without your mobile phone?

In the past, people wouldn't go as far away on holiday as they do today. **use**
In the past, people ...... as far away on holiday as they do today.

This town has a lot more cinemas than it had in the past. **used**
This town ...... so many cinemas.
items.answer: never used to have/used not to have

sections.title: I: Error correction (extra words)
sections.instructions: Find the extra word in each line.

items.sentence: Childhood would used to be quite different from what it is today.
Young people didn't use not to have so much leisure time.
Today's children may complain about their schoolwork, but our great-grandparents would to go out to work at a very young age.
They had often been left school by the time they were fourteen and were found a job.
This meant that they have had little free time for hobbies or leisure activities, especially when they had been working hard all day.
Of course, they got themselves used to working long hours eventually, but it would meant that they had to grow up very quickly.
Today, we are got used to having some free time to do things we enjoy, a luxury people in the past rarely were had.

sections.title: J: Cloze
sections.instructions: Write one word in each gap.

items.sentence: Jane lay awake. She had (1) ...... preparing for the next day (2) ...... a long time and now she couldn't sleep.
Her team (3) ...... playing the local champions at water polo in the final and Jane was the captain.
She (4) ...... feeling the pressure.
She turned over and remembered how she (5) ...... learned to swim.
They (6) ...... go to the local pool every day after school and her father (7) ...... to show her what to do.
She hadn't liked the water at first, but she soon (8) ...... used to it.
She had (9) ...... their youngest member!
She quickly got used to scoring goals and (10) ...... under pressure, but tomorrow was different.
(11) ...... used to have problems sleeping, she thought to herself.
But then again, I didn't (12) ...... to be the captain of the team.
